standard_description: Explain the function of conjunctions, prepositions, and interjections in general and their function in particular sentences.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Recognizing fragments
khan_exercises.description: A fragment is a part of a sentence, but can't be a sentence on its own.

items.question_content:
**Which choice is a complete sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

The complete sentence is

>**The winter is cold.**

The other choice is a fragment.

choices.content: In the wintertime.
The winter is cold.

items.question_content:
**Which choice is a complete sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

The complete sentence is

>**I went to the baseball game.**

The other choice is a fragment.

choices.content: I went to the baseball game.
Instead of going to the baseball game.

items.question_content:
**Which choice is a complete sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

The complete sentence is

choices.content: What are we eating for dinner?

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"Since I love being on the lake" is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**I went kayaking. I love being on the lake.**

choices.content: I went kayaking. Since I love being on the lake.
I went kayaking. I love being on the lake.

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"Instead of going to the party" is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**Instead of going to the party, I stayed home and watched an I Love Lucy marathon.**

choices.content: Instead of going to the party, I stayed home and watched an I Love Lucy marathon.
Instead of going to the party. I stayed home and watched an I Love Lucy marathon.

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"While you were out of town" is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**While you were out of town, I watered your plants.**

choices.content: While you were out of town. I watered your plants.
While you were out of town, I watered your plants.

items.question_content:

hints:

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"Because tickets on a first-come, first-served basis." is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**Be there on time! Tickets are given on a first-come, first-served basis.**

choices.content: Be there on time! Because tickets on a first-come, first-served basis.
Be there on time! Tickets are given on a first-come, first-served basis.

choices.content: Just as I said. We’re going to the carnival.

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"While usually on time." is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"Depicting water lilies in many of his works of art." is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**Monet was an impressionist painter. He depicted water lilies in many of his works of art.**

choices.content: Monet was an impressionist painter. He depicted water lilies in many of his works of art.
Monet was an impressionist painter. Depicting water lilies in many of his works of art.

items.question_content:
**Which choice uses only complete sentences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **fragment** is a piece of a sentence that cannot stand on its own but incorrectly ends in a period.

Remember that a sentence must contain an **independent clause** to be complete!

"After the storm passed" is a sentence fragment, so the choice that uses only complete sentences is

>**We carefully stepped outside. The storm had passed.**

choices.content: After the storm passed. We carefully stepped outside.
We carefully stepped outside. The storm had passed.

khan_exercises.title: Recognizing run-ons and comma splices
khan_exercises.description: Run-on sentences and comma splices happen when a writer isn't careful with commas. Let's see how you do!

items.question_content:
**Choose the answer that contains no run-on or comma splice errors.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Independent clauses** can be joined in two ways:

* comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS)

 *I went to the store, but I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

* semicolon

 *I went to the store; however, I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

If we don't one of these methods, we create a grammar error:

* A **run-on sentence** mushes two independent clauses together with no punctuation.

 *I went to the store I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

* A **comma splice** uses a comma, but forgets to use a conjunction.

 *I went to the store, I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

The grammatical choice is 

>**I'm going to the store to buy apples.**

The other choice is a run-on sentence.

choices.content: I'm going to the store to buy apples.
I'm going to the store I need to buy apples.

items.question_content:
**Choose the answer that contains no run-on or comma splice errors.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Independent clauses** can be joined in two ways:

* comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS)

 *I went to the store, but I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

* semicolon

 *I went to the store; however, I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

If we don't one of these methods, we create a grammar error:

* A **run-on sentence** mushes two independent clauses together with no punctuation.

 *I went to the store I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

* A **comma splice** uses a comma, but forgets to use a conjunction.

 *I went to the store, I forgot to buy peanut butter.*

The grammatical choice is 

>**My favorite animals are chickens, bears, and owls.**

The other choice is a run-on sentence.

choices.content: My favorite animals are chickens, bears, and owls.
My favorite animals are chickens and bears and I also like owls.
